title: ReliaQuest joins OpenAI Daybreak, gets access to GPT-5.5-Cyber for enterprise defense
subtitle: The Tampa-based SOC platform is now a named partner in OpenAI's cyber program, bringing frontier cyber models into GreyMatter's agentic defense workflow.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: ReliaQuest announced on July 20 that it is joining the OpenAI Daybreak Cyber Partner Program, becoming one of the first security vendors to formally embed OpenAI's frontier cyber models into an enterprise SOC platform. Under the partnership, ReliaQuest gets access to OpenAI's GPT-5.5-Cyber model and Trusted Access for Cyber capabilities, which it will pipe into its GreyMatter platform for agentic detection, investigation, and response. The two companies said they will jointly select and prioritize use cases considered most impactful to enterprise security operations, and will collaborate on testing and deployment.

The Daybreak program is OpenAI's push into applied cybersecurity — a set of tools and access rights announced this summer for a small group of security vendors and defenders. The offering includes GPT-5.5-Cyber (a specialized model for trusted defenders), a Codex Security plugin for finding and validating vulnerabilities in code, and the Trusted Access for Cyber tier that lets partners use these tools inside their own commercial products. ReliaQuest's role gives Daybreak reach into the SOC-as-a-service segment, where large enterprises outsource day-to-day threat detection to a managed provider. GreyMatter already runs across Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, CrowdStrike, and other stacks, so the integration lands in front of a broad enterprise customer base rather than a single tool's user list.

The move fits a broader industry pattern. AI security acquisitions tripled from 10 in the first half of 2025 to 29 in the first half of 2026, and Hugging Face's disclosure last week — an autonomous agent-driven breach that its own blue team couldn't investigate at frontier-model speed because provider safety guardrails blocked the exploit payloads — showed exactly the gap Daybreak is designed to fill. The current defensive AI stack was built around helpful-assistant safety filters, and those filters cannot tell a legitimate incident responder from an attacker. Purpose-built cyber models with Trusted Access controls, gated to vetted partners like ReliaQuest, are the industry's first real attempt to solve that.

For learners: cybersecurity is going to be one of the biggest AI job categories over the next five years, and the barrier to entry is lower than most fields. If you have curiosity about how systems break, the free CTF (capture-the-flag) circuit, tryhackme.com, and HackTheBox will teach you more in six months than any degree — and AI security specifically is short-staffed everywhere. Attackers are already using LLMs to write phishing, generate exploit code, and automate reconnaissance. The defenders need people who understand both sides. Learn the basics, then learn how AI reshapes them.
